notes: A real, confirmed advantage over Chattanooga/Hamilton County's equivalent check — the documented reason Asheville's Room for others score sits above Chattanooga's
The national $687-750 figure is not location-specific

Real, material conflict with crowdsourced-aggregator framing, which paints Asheville as notably above-average; the Chamber's own PDF cites a 95.6-106.2 range dating to 1990 — historically always near the national middle
Absolute-level crime-rate sourcing conflicts by up to 2.5x depending on methodology, but the improving trajectory holds regardless of which baseline is used
Unlike Rincón's explicit 30-40 minute Mayagüez finding, no ER-distance/hospital-access fact was found in Asheville's own files this pass

AVL Digital Nomads, dedicated coworking spaces, and nomad-guide-site profiles all confirm a real, organic scene — but no quantified resident/remote-worker count was found, unlike Mérida's (4,000-6,000) or Oaxaca's (~22,700) sourced figures

Local reporting explicitly names displacement risk to "established residents and artists" — same mechanism as foreign-driven gentrification elsewhere, just without the "anti-gringo" framing since it's domestic
43 dead in Buncombe County alone; recovery real but incomplete — reservoir fuse gate not fully repaired until fall 2026, ~130 people still homeless

A separate Canopy MLS (real closed-transaction) figure runs materially higher ($452,550 median, $569,448 mean) — a genuine, unreconciled sourcing conflict that makes the budget-fit problem look worse, not better
Down 2.86% year-over-year (softening trend, not a sharp drop)

Earlier research flagged this as possibly a non-issue under a domestic-relocation framing, the way Rincón's turned out to be — that framing premise is corrected as of 2026-07-08: the foreign-national framing applies here, so an actual international customs crossing likely IS the real scenario, not a non-issue. Still genuinely unresearched either way — flagging the corrected premise, not assuming the answer
No dedicated search done on NC's political climate or culture-war-adjacent local ordinances
Buncombe County-wide 2026 reassessment; property tax here is not a flat, predictable number
5 sources genuinely disagree by a wide band
Two independent searches converge — what exists in the sub-$150k tier is manufactured housing or vacant land, not a comparable standalone home
Confirmed no looser county-level option, unlike Buncombe County's real exception — the documented reason Chattanooga's score sits below Asheville's

165+ listings citywide under $150k, including a real 3BR/1BA bungalow at $69,900 — the closest realistic in-budget match found anywhere in USA's research; caveat: correlates specifically with a historically disinvested neighborhood, not a like-for-like discount
Same national-figure-only gap as Asheville
Reads as genuinely calmer day-to-day, but rests on a thinner dedicated search than Asheville's — not confirmed-clean
No offsetting improving-trend data found the way Asheville's APD 2025 report provided
Same gap as Asheville's — no ER-distance/hospital-access fact found in Chattanooga's own files this pass
Vs. 12.5% national; +2 points 2022-2025; 32% of workers hold jobs paying below the local cost-of-basics threshold despite a real manufacturing jobs boom
Chattanooga's own community-network.md wasn't checked for a countable-population figure this pass
Genuinely below Asheville's range at either end of either city's own spread
Vs. North Carolina's flat 3.99% (Asheville) — a genuine, structural cost-of-living difference
13% below the US national average overall; housing specifically 23% below
Same reasoning as Asheville's row
Same gap as Asheville's
Numbeo and Expatistan genuinely agree once compared like-for-like (~4% apart on an all-in basis)
Structural legal exclusion, not a pricing quirk — Puerto Rico residents cannot use healthcare.gov at all

fact_label: Act 60 Individual Resident Investor accepts pre-existing passive income
notes: The 0% treatment applies only to gains/income accrued after establishing PR residency, not pre-existing passive income; the separate Export Services track (services billed to non-PR clients) is the only route that could apply to pre-existing income, and only if restructured to meet that track's own requirements

fact_label: Act 60 Individual Resident Investor decree — application window closing
notes: Applicants after Dec 31, 2026 face a new 4% tax on post-residency investment income instead of 0%, plus a 6-year non-PR-residency look-back

33% energy shortfall in 2025; Hurricane Fiona (2022) knocked out the grid for up to 4 weeks in parts of the island — a chronic, not one-time, vulnerability, "suspended between two realities" per a top regulator

Worse statistically than either mainland location, but qualitative sources describe it as generally safe day-to-day — the same "worse on paper, calmer in practice" pattern this project flagged for Lagos
No 24-hour ER in Rincón itself; nearest is Mayagüez — meaningfully shorter than Atitlán's 2.5-3 hour buffer, but not "there's a hospital in town"
No quantified count of Act-60 arrivals or mainland transplants found for Rincón specifically, unlike Mérida's or Oaxaca's sourced figures

Rincón's organized backlash (row above) is a social/political mechanism (protest, messaging); this is a distinct criminal-targeting mechanism, not searched for separately — the two shouldn't be read as one finding covering both
Act 60 arrivals directly named as backlash targets in national reporting; the sharpest organized social-conflict finding anywhere in USA's research
Given the domestic-travel framing above, a cat-specific divergence is less likely than for an international candidate, but not yet confirmed either way

fact_label: Pet import (dog) — rabies titer / quarantine requirement
value_raw: Domestic travel, not international — standard rabies/health certificate, microchip, parasite treatment, no quarantine, no import permit
notes: Puerto Rico is domestic US travel for pet purposes — genuinely simpler than Guatemala's or Mexico's requirements
$76B in restructuring savings, first balanced budget passed June 2025, but three more required before the board's mandate can even conclude
Currently favorable relative to Asheville's reassessment shock, but PR's own fiscal oversight board has flagged the system as needing an overhaul — a low bill today isn't necessarily stable long-term
Not closed with a dedicated search the way Asheville's and Chattanooga's both were; the overall price level suggests this gap may be even more material here
Whether a given applicant's income sits above or below this line determines ACA subsidy eligibility — the single biggest swing factor in this cost category across all three USA locations

value_raw: Not tolerated — a visible pattern is itself a risk trigger
notes: Named explicitly as this project's "notably stricter than most candidates" case: no CA-4-style shared clock or tolerated workaround exists; CBP officers can deny re-entry or shorten admission on suspicion of a repeat short-exit pattern
Lowest closing-cost range of any candidate in this project

The headline Framing-B finding, unchanged: unlike Guatemala, Colombia, Argentina, Portugal, Greece, and Mexico — each of which has some passive-income/remote-work category — the US has never created one. The flip side, now developed: most real routes that do exist convert directly to permanent residency, unlike a renewable-forever nomad visa

value_raw: ~55,000 green cards/year, random lottery, excludes high-volume source countries
notes: Named for completeness, not developed as a plannable route; whether this project's own candidate countries are excluded by recent admission volumes was not checked country-by-country — a real, named gap

fact_label: E-2 Treaty Investor — nationality-restricted (treaty country required)
notes: The applicant's own nationality, not residence, gates eligibility entirely — a structural cap the EB-5/NIW/EB-1A routes don't share

fact_label: E-2 Treaty Investor — qualifying investment threshold
notes: No fixed statutory minimum — a "substantial," at-risk investment scaled to the business. Genuinely the lower-capital investor option this pass was asked to find, well below EB-5's floor

fact_label: E-2 Treaty Investor converts to permanent residency
notes: Renewable indefinitely as a non-immigrant status but does not itself lead to a green card — the honest catch behind the lower capital bar; a green card requires separately qualifying under EB-5, EB-1C, or another category

fact_label: EB-1A Extraordinary Ability — self-petition, no employer/PERM required
notes: Requires sustained national/international acclaim (3 of 10 evidentiary criteria); converts directly to a green card, same immigrant-category shape as NIW but a materially higher evidentiary bar — "very top of the field," not just a strong professional record

fact_label: EB-2 India — final action date backlog
notes: The single most easily-overlooked fact in this whole section: identical category, identical filing date, wildly different outcome by country of birth (not citizenship or residence)

fact_label: EB-2 National Interest Waiver (NIW) — self-petition, no employer/PERM required
notes: Requires advanced degree or exceptional ability, plus the Dhanasar 3-prong national-interest test; converts directly to a green card (this IS the immigrant category, not a temporary status). Best fit for a professional/research-track-record profile, a weak fit for pure passive/rental income with no distinguishing achievement

fact_label: EB-2 NIW — I-140 premium processing fee (2026)
notes: Effective March 1, 2026; buys a 45-business-day USCIS adjudication commitment. Standard processing ran 2.5-26.5 months for 80% of cases as of June 2026

fact_label: EB-2/EB-3 backlog — rest of world (most nationalities, including this project's own candidate countries)
notes: The large majority of the world's nationalities, including Guatemalan-, Colombian-, Moroccan-, and Thai-born applicants, face no comparable structural wait — a real, material "which passport" fact

fact_label: EB-3 India — final action date backlog
notes: Same per-country-cap mechanism as EB-2 India above

fact_label: EB-5 Investor Visa — qualifying investment threshold
notes: $800k in a Targeted Employment Area (rural/high-unemployment) or qualifying infrastructure project, $1.05M elsewhere; USCIS states these hold through FY2026, inflation-indexed increase expected from January 2027 (trajectory qualifier: stable through FY2026 specifically)

fact_label: EB-5 Investor Visa accepts passive income as qualifying proof
notes: An asset/capital-investment test, not an income test — same shape as Guatemala's Investor Visa row

fact_label: EB-5 Investor Visa converts to permanent residency
notes: 2-year conditional green card first, I-829 petition to remove conditions once the 10-job requirement is proven, then unconditional; citizenship-eligible after 5 years total residency

fact_label: Family sponsorship — F1 preference (unmarried adult child of a citizen) backlog
notes: Real, converts directly to permanent residency, but genuinely too slow to plan a move around for most applicants

fact_label: Family sponsorship — F3 preference (married child of a citizen) backlog
notes: A Filipino family that filed in 2005 only saw its priority date become current in April 2026 — a real 21-year worked example, not a projection

fact_label: Family sponsorship — immediate relative of a US citizen (spouse/parent/minor child)
value_raw: No cap, no backlog (~12-24 months all-in)
notes: Genuinely one of the fastest, least-capital routes in the entire system — requires an actual qualifying relationship, not creatable on demand

China, Russia, Iran, North Korea, Cuba, Venezuela, Syria recur across enacted state laws; mostly scoped to agricultural land, critical-infrastructure proximity, or land near military bases, not a blanket ban — narrower than Guatemala's/Mexico's restricted zones but real and nationality-dependent, a fact Framing A's "no restriction at all" summary couldn't surface

fact_label: H-1B — supplemental fee for consular-processing petitions
notes: Applies only when the beneficiary is outside the US at filing; change-of-status filings from inside the US (e.g. F-1 to H-1B) are exempt — an easily-missed distinction
H-1B is a dual-intent visa — pursuing a green card doesn't jeopardize the underlying status, unlike a B-visa

fact_label: H-1B employer-sponsored visa — lottery selection odds (FY2027)
notes: New wage-weighted lottery: ~15% at entry-level (Level I) wage up to ~46%+ at the top (Level IV) tier — not a single flat probability. Requires an actual US employer/specialty-occupation job offer, not a fit for remote/passive income alone
Projected +8% further in 2026; high wind-risk areas +58%

fact_label: International Entrepreneur Rule — status type and duration
value_raw: Parole (not a visa), up to 2.5 years, renewable once to 5-year max
notes: Discretionary, revocable, no formal immigration status; a new $1,000 parole fee applies as of October 16, 2025

fact_label: International Entrepreneur Rule converts to permanent residency
notes: The lowest personal-capital bar of any route in this file, and the most fragile — parole recipients generally must separately qualify under another category, sometimes after leaving the US, to ever adjust to permanent residency

fact_label: International Entrepreneur Rule (IER) — qualifying startup investment
notes: No personal-investment minimum — the startup itself must meet this bar. Largest update since the program's 2017 launch

source_ref: [N/A — domestic relocation under Framing A, not a foreign-national destination]
notes: This mechanism (as distinct from organized social backlash, which Rincón's file does document) doesn't apply the same way it does at every other candidate in this project — a US citizen relocating domestically isn't a foreign national in a local market. Stated explicitly as N/A rather than left silent

fact_label: L-1A intracompany transferee → EB-1C — converts to permanent residency
notes: Typical timeline 3-5 years for most nationalities, among the faster employment-based routes — but only usable by someone who already owns/executively runs a genuine, operating foreign company capable of a real US affiliate; not a route buildable from scratch
Largest single-year FBI-recorded drop ever (2021→2024); 2025 estimate would be the lowest ever recorded since 1900

O-1 is a real non-immigrant bridge to EB-1A/NIW in practice but requires an employer/agent sponsor unlike the two self-petition routes above; whether it permits dual intent without jeopardizing status was not confirmed

fact_label: Standard EB-2/EB-3 (PERM, employer-sponsored) converts to permanent residency
notes: Immigrant category by construction — the per-country backlog below is a wait for an already-guaranteed outcome, not a risk of denial

value_raw: 180d-1yr: 3-year bar; 1yr+: 10-year bar; VWP overstay: possible permanent VWP ineligibility + no right to contest removal before a judge
notes: VWP overstay carries a harsher structural consequence than a B-visa overstay — a real asymmetry between the two visitor tracks

fact_label: Tourist/visitor stay length — B-1/B-2 (non-VWP nationalities, the majority of the world)
notes: Covers most nationalities, including every one of this project's own candidate countries; visa document itself often valid up to 10 years multiple-entry, but each admitted stay is set by the CBP officer at entry, typically up to 6 months
Automatic on arrival via ESTA, no consular interview, for ~42 countries (most of Western Europe, UK, Japan, South Korea, Australia, NZ, Chile, Singapore, Taiwan, Israel, others)

fact_label: Tourist/visitor time counts toward anything longer-term
notes: Sharper than the standard "doesn't count" default found elsewhere: nonimmigrant intent is an active, sworn requirement, so filing for a green card too soon after a tourist entry can itself be read as evidence of misrepresented intent at entry — a fraud-adjacent risk, not just a wasted clock

value_raw: VWP: none (narrow 30-day medical exception only); B-2: one, up to +6 months
notes: B-2 extension filed via Form I-539, 45+ days before expiry, discretionary, requires a legitimate reason; no second extension except extraordinary circumstances
Roughly doubled after enhanced subsidies expired Dec 31, 2025 — a real, quantified, recent cost shift with no equivalent anywhere else in this project
